text: The surgeon identifies the pars flaccida located just above the caudate lobe of the liver, highlighting its anatomical position in the surgical field.
The surgeon opens the peritoneum to facilitate the anterior mobilization of the esophagus, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon lifts the peritoneum using a harmonic scalpel, facilitating the anterior mobilization of the esophagus.

The surgeon opens the peritoneum over the pars flaccida, then proceeds to dissect the anterior peritoneal from right to left. The vena cava is visible just below the pars flaccida and beneath the caudate lobe of the liver.
The surgeon identifies the right crus of the diaphragm during the laparoscopic Heller's myotomy procedure.
The surgeon stretches the peritoneum downward to prevent injury to the anterior vagus nerve, ensuring a proper fascial plane is visible.
The surgeon progresses the dissection towards the angle of His, focusing on the left side of the gastroesophageal junction.
The spleen is visible on the extreme left side of the surgical field.
The surgeon continues the dissection, revealing the esophagus clearly within the surgical field.
The surgeon dissects the peritoneum over the right crus, carefully exposing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon holds the fundus of the stomach and stretches it downward to reveal the peritoneal plane. The peritoneum over the left crus is visibly separated.
The surgeon cuts at least five centimeters of the muscular layer of the esophagus both circularly and longitudinally, ensuring proper mobilization for the cardiomyotomy procedure.
The liver retractor is repositioned to enhance exposure, facilitating the mobilization of the esophagus.
The right crus of the diaphragm is visible in the surgical field, with surrounding tissue dissected to expose this anatomical structure.
The surgeon begins the posterior mobilization of the esophagus, initially separating it from the right crus using careful dissection techniques.

A small gauze piece is used to manage minor oozing and assist in blunt dissection. The surgeon stretches the muscle using an atraumatic grasper and Maryland dissector, separating the longitudinal fibers until the mucosa bulges, while carefully avoiding perforation of the oesophagus.
The surgeon stretches and splits the muscle layers of the esophagus using atraumatic graspers, without needing to use a hook, as the layers are fine.
The surgeon exposes the mucosa beneath the muscle layer during the cardiomytomy procedure, revealing the underlying tissue structure.
The surgeon addresses minor oozing by swabbing the area, applying pressure to control bleeding, while minimizing the use of energy devices.
The surgeon observes the intra-abdominal length of the oesophagus, noting that extensive mobilization is not necessary due to its adequate length.
At the gastroesophageal junction, the surgeon carefully manipulates the circular muscle fibers, ensuring they are properly joined at this level.
The surgeon continues to slit the muscle fibers, progressively exposing the underlying mucosa at the surgical site.
The surgeon addresses the residual muscle fibers at the gastroesophageal junction, ensuring careful removal to prevent damage to the underlying mucosa.
The surgeon applies pressure to the surgical site to stop bleeding, minimizing the use of energy devices.
The surgeon begins suturing the left side of the crura using either 3-0 or 2-0 Ethibond suture material.
The surgeon takes a suture bite through the left split longitudinal muscle of the esophagus, following the exposure of the mucosa.
The surgeon applies the second knot in a reverse C pattern during the suturing process.
Scissors are introduced from the left side to trim the suture tails.
The surgeon uses a Nethacin's lever retractor to lift and adjust the tissue for better exposure. A new suture is introduced, limited to a length of twenty centimeters for intracorporeal suturing.
The surgeon readjusts the retractor to enhance exposure of the surgical field. The omentum is visibly trapped by the left instrument, requiring careful manipulation.
The surgeon continues with the anterior partial wrap, known as the Dor fundoplication, using visible sutures to secure the wrap around the esophagus.
The surgeon carefully manipulates the right cross, ensuring it does not damage the adjacent vena cava during the cardiomytomy procedure.
The surgeon uses a grasper to pull anteriorly while tightening a knot, opting for a sudden knot due to reduced traction, with the option to use a tumble square knot if desired.
The surgeon takes a bite through the right side of the esophageal muscle and the right crus simultaneously.

The surgeon uses a Maryland knot with an assisting needle holder to secure the anterior partial wrap. Care is taken to ensure the wrap does not get trapped in the joints of the Maryland instrument before tying the knot.
The surgeon tightens the final knot in the cardiomyotomy procedure, completing the suturing process.
